Replace.Text: Czechia
Czechia
Czech
Ostsiedlung

Non-capital cities founded on Hills start with 2 [ICON_Citizen] Population and receive +1 [ICON_Culture] Culture until unlocking the Nationalism civic. After unlocking Nationalism, these cities receive -5 Loyalty per turn.
Ottokar II

King Ottokar, iron in war and golden in ambition, the crown of Czechia is yours to raise. Let your riders carry your banners across the field, let the mines of your realm fill your coffers, and let the towns of Czechia grow rich beside river, hill, and harvest. Build a kingdom worthy of legend, and history will remember you as the king who made Czechia a power among empires.

Mighty Matthias, the raven banner flies again over the lands of Czechia. Govern these hills, towns, and fortresses with discipline and cunning, and let your soldiers answer every threat with steel. If your rule is firm and your alliances wise, your enemies will learn that the Black Army casts a long shadow.
The Iron and Golden King
+30% [ICON_Production] Production toward Heavy Cavalry units. Mines over Bonus, Luxury, and Strategic resources provide +1 [ICON_Gold] Gold.
Beer
Brewery

Unique Czech improvement unlocked with Guilds. Must be built on Wheat near Fresh Water. Provides +1 [ICON_Gold] Gold, +1 [ICON_Food] Food, +0.5 Housing, and 1 [ICON_RESOURCE_BEER] Beer, a unique Luxury resource. Receives +1 [ICON_Faith] Faith for each adjacent Holy Site, +1 [ICON_Gold] Gold for each adjacent Commercial Hub or Harbor, and +1 [ICON_Food] Food from every 2 adjacent Farms. Receives +1 [ICON_Food] Food with Mass Production and +2 [ICON_Gold] Gold with Chemistry.
+{1_num} [ICON_Faith] Faith from the adjacent Holy Site.
+{1_num} [ICON_Gold] Gold from the adjacent Commercial Hub.
+{1_num} [ICON_Gold] Gold from the adjacent Harbor.
+{1_num} [ICON_Food] Food from every 2 adjacent Farms.
Hussite War Wagon

Czech unique ranged unit unlocked with the Reformed Church civic. Stronger than the Crossbowman but weaker than the Field Cannon. Gains +10 [ICON_Strength] Combat Strength when defending while fortified, and an additional +3 [ICON_Strength] Combat Strength for each adjacent Hussite War Wagon when defending. Upgrades into the Field Cannon.
Wagenburg
+10 [ICON_Strength] Combat Strength when defending while fortified, and +3 [ICON_Strength] Combat Strength for each adjacent Hussite War Wagon when defending.
+{1_Amount} [ICON_Strength] Combat Strength from fortified Wagenburg.
+{1_Amount} [ICON_Strength] Combat Strength from adjacent Hussite War Wagons.
Prague
Brno
Ostrava
Plzeň
Olomouc
Liberec
Hradec Králové
České Budějovice
Pardubice
Zlín
Jihlava
Karlovy Vary
Tábor
Kutná Hora
Znojmo
Opava
Kroměříž
Uherské Hradiště
Přerov
Prostějov
Třebíč
Nový Jičín
Frýdek-Místek
Karviná
Těšín
Krnov
Bruntál
Hodonín
Mikulov
Vltava River
Labe River
Morava River
Dyje River
Ohře River
Sázava River
Lužnice River
Odra River
Krkonoše Mountains
Šumava Mountains
Beskydy Mountains
Jeseníky Mountains
Orlické Mountains
Krušné Mountains
Bílé Karpaty Mountains
Českomoravská vrchovina Mountains
Černé jezero
Čertovo jezero
Plešné jezero
Prášilské jezero
Jezero Laka
Odlezelské jezero
Komorní hůrka
Železná hůrka
Milešovka
Říp
Trosky
I am Matthias. I am King of Czechia, scourge of my foes, and defender of the faithful. Greetings.
I will personally lead your delegates on a tour of my capital, that they may understand the power of Czechia more fully.
Yes. I agree to your terms on behalf of Czechia.
I invite your people to learn of our capital, but only if you grant me the same right.
Yes! There are few rulers who understand the burden of kings as well as you. I am glad to call you friend.
A king must choose his friends with great caution. It is not time for me to call you that.
Do not we both rule with the acclaim of our people and the will of God? Should this not bind us as true friends before the world?
Yes, thanks be to God.
This is not possible.
Surely you had to know that one day there would be war between us.
Your insolence has provoked this war, and I shall be happy to lead the Black Army to victory over you.
Thank you for the gifts of your delegation, and may your generosity be blessed.
We need no gifts nor their implicit obligations. We are rich enough.
I am sending you gifts worthy of a prince: Fine lace, a crossbow emblazoned with your heraldry, and paprikash made from my family's recipe.
You are a lawless snake, a grasping vulture, who would see the world cower before you. Know that I will never fear you!
You pursue power—always and only power—but Czechia will resist you to the bitter end.
Your return honors me.
You respect the rights and authority of Czechia, and have proven a worthy friend. Let us cement our bond with an alliance.
Alright, I think we've both proven our points. Now let us move on to mercy and peace.
Never, never.
There is neither mercy in prolonging this war nor justice in pursuing the causes. Let us make peace.
Yes, let this be so. Thank you for the right of open passage.
This would be an imposition, and an unacceptable one at that.
Grant that we may have the rights of movement across your borders, and I will extend the same to you.
The scouts of my armies have spent days following the progress of your armies near the border. Move them away from Czechia.
Today you wear the victor's laurels, but can you keep what you have taken?
It would be beneath a prince's dignity to accept this offer.
A generous offer! I accept with a grateful heart.
The quickest way to success is to levy the strengths of your little allies, wouldn't you agree?
They say you pay your auxiliaries in patriotism. That and two pennies will buy them a beer.
I am Ottokar, King of Czechia, a ruler of iron in war and gold in peace. Come in friendship, and you will find honor at my court.
Let our envoys see your capital, and yours shall be received in our great capital with fitting honor.
Very well. I accept these terms in the name of Czechia.
Reveal the road to your capital, and I shall reveal the road to our great capital in return.
Yes. A wise ruler knows when another crown is worth trusting.
Not yet. Friendship between rulers must be forged, not merely spoken.
Let our crowns stand together before the world. Will you accept my friendship?
Then let it be known: our friendship stands before God and history.
No. The hour is not right for such a bond.
So, the mask falls. You will learn that Czechia does not bend beneath threats.
I have weighed your insults and ambitions long enough. Now Czechia rides to war.
Your gifts are received with honor. May courtesy open the way to wiser dealings.
No. Gifts too often arrive with hooks hidden beneath the ribbon.
I send you gifts from Czechia: silverwork, strong beer, fine horses, and cloth worthy of a royal hall.
Call me what you will. Lesser rulers often mistake strength for tyranny.
You dress greed in noble words, but I see the hunger beneath. Czechia will remember this insult.
You return to my court. Speak, and let us see what may be settled.
You have proven strong, honorable, and useful. Let our realms be bound by alliance.
Good. Let pride give way to prudence, and let peace return.
No. You have not yet paid the price this war demands.
Enough blood has watered this quarrel. Let us end it with terms worthy of kings.
Yes. Let your people pass, so long as they remember whose roads they travel.
No. Czechia's roads are not opened merely because another ruler asks.
Open your roads to my people, and I shall open mine to yours.
Your soldiers gather too close to Czechia. Withdraw them, unless you mean to confess your intentions.
You have taken my crown's fortune today. Guard it well, for history is rarely gentle to conquerors.
This offer is thin coin for a heavy matter. I refuse.
A fair bargain. Czechia accepts.
Your mines are well kept, and your coffers do not lie idle. A ruler should know the worth beneath the earth.
You leave wealth buried and strength unused. A kingdom that neglects its mines neglects its future.

The lands of Czechia lie at the heart of Europe, where rivers, forests, mountains, and trade roads shaped a long history of towns, castles, mines, monasteries, and contested crowns. Bohemia and Moravia became meeting places for German, Slavic, Latin, and later Hussite traditions, often flourishing precisely because they stood between larger powers.

In the early Middle Ages, the Přemyslid rulers built the Duchy and later Kingdom of Bohemia into one of the most important states of the Holy Roman Empire. Prague grew as a political and cultural center, while the hills and borderlands supported fortified settlements, mining towns, and expanding agriculture.

The movement of settlers into hill country and frontier regions changed the landscape of Czechia. New towns, fields, and mining communities strengthened royal authority and brought wealth, but also tied the country to wider Central European currents of law, language, commerce, and faith.

In the fifteenth century, the Hussite Wars placed Czech lands at the center of religious and military upheaval. Wagon forts, disciplined infantry, and a fierce defense of reform made the Hussites feared by armies far larger and wealthier than their own.

Czechia's history is one of rich towns, stubborn frontiers, learned courts, religious conflict, and survival between empires. Its strength came from making difficult ground productive, turning silver and craft into power, and defending its cities with a will that larger neighbors could not easily break.

Ottokar II Přemysl, king of Bohemia from 1253 to 1278, was one of the most powerful rulers in Central Europe during the thirteenth century. His contemporaries remembered him as the "Iron and Golden King" - iron for his military strength, and golden for the wealth and splendor of his court.

Through inheritance, marriage, diplomacy, and war, Ottokar extended his rule far beyond Bohemia. At the height of his power, his influence reached across Austria, Styria, Carinthia, Carniola, and other lands, making him a serious contender in imperial politics.

Ottokar promoted towns, trade, fortifications, and royal authority. His reign helped connect Czechia more tightly to the political and economic world of Central Europe, while the wealth of mines and cities supported his ambitions.

His rivalry with Rudolf of Habsburg ended in defeat at the Battle on the Marchfeld in 1278. Even so, Ottokar's reign marked a high point of Přemyslid power, and his reputation as a brilliant, ambitious, and dangerous king endured long after his death.
Ottokar II favors production, mines, gold, and heavy cavalry. Use Czechia's hill settlements and resource mines to build a wealthy realm, then convert that wealth into fast military pressure.

Ottokar's ability rewards improving resources with Mines and building Heavy Cavalry. Settle near hills and mineable resources, then use the extra [ICON_Gold] Gold to support expansion, upgrades, and warfare. Czechia's Ostsiedlung helps hill cities grow quickly before Nationalism, while the Brewery adds food, gold, housing, and the Beer luxury to make compact inland development more valuable.

Matthias Corvinus ruled Hungary from 1458 to 1490 and became one of the most formidable monarchs of late medieval Central Europe. In this Czechia configuration, he represents his historical struggle for influence in Moravia, Silesia, and Bohemia, where his armies and diplomacy brought him into direct conflict with the politics of the Czech crown.

Matthias was renowned for the Black Army, a professional military force that gave him unusual flexibility and striking power. His ambitions reached into Austria and the Czech lands, making him both a rival and a claimant in the tangled politics that followed the Hussite era.

His court also became famous for Renaissance learning and royal display. Matthias cultivated scholars, books, diplomacy, and military prestige, projecting the image of a ruler whose authority rested on both arms and intellect.
Matthias combines Czechia's strong hill settlement and Brewery economy with the Raven King style of military opportunism.

Playing Czechia under Matthias rewards disciplined expansion into hills and careful timing. Use Ostsiedlung to establish productive non-capital cities, then lean on Czechia's unique infrastructure and defensive Hussite War Wagons while Matthias' military strengths help turn city-state and alliance politics into battlefield pressure.

During the Hussite Wars of the fifteenth century, Czech commanders used wagon forts to offset the advantages of heavily armored knights and crusading armies. Ordinary wagons were reinforced, chained together, and arranged into mobile defensive positions from which soldiers could fire crossbows, handguns, and artillery.

The wagenburg was not merely a barricade. Properly handled, it became a disciplined tactical system: infantry, missile troops, guns, and counterattacks all worked from the shelter of the wagons. This made Hussite armies dangerous even when outnumbered.

In Civilization VI, the Hussite War Wagon is strongest when it holds ground. Fortify it and support it with nearby War Wagons to create a defensive line that punishes attacks and protects key cities or advances.

Beer has been brewed in Czech lands for centuries, from monasteries and royal towns to local burgher breweries. Access to grain, clean water, and urban markets helped make brewing an important craft and a lasting part of Czech social life.

Medieval towns often guarded brewing rights closely, since beer brought both food value and income. Breweries tied agriculture, trade, and civic privilege together, turning harvests into a product that could support towns, travelers, and local treasuries.

In Civilization VI, the Brewery works best beside farms and commercial or religious districts. It gives Czechia food, gold, housing, and eventually the unique Beer luxury, making it a compact improvement for supporting growing inland cities.

Beer is one of the oldest prepared drinks in Europe, and in Czech lands it became especially closely tied to town life, agriculture, and craft privilege. Barley, wheat, hops, and water all shaped local brewing traditions.

By the late Middle Ages and early modern period, brewing rights could be a valuable source of civic wealth. The drink was consumed daily, traded locally, and treated as a serious economic good rather than a mere luxury of feasts.
